title: Owd Roïŋ―(1)
content:
NAïŋ―Y, noïŋ― mander (2) oïŋ― use to be callinïŋ― ïŋ―im Roïŋ―, Roïŋ―, Roïŋ―,
Fur the dogïŋ―s stoïŋ―n-deaf, anïŋ― eïŋ―s blind, ïŋ―e can naither Stanïŋ― nor goïŋ―.

But I means fur to maïŋ―ke ïŋ―is owd aïŋ―ge as ïŋ―appy as iver I can,
Fur I owïŋ―s owd Roïŋ―ver moor nor I iver owïŋ―d mottal man.

Thouïŋ―s rode of ïŋ―is back when a babby, afoor thou was gotten too owd,
Fur ïŋ―eïŋ―d fetch anïŋ― carry like owt, ïŋ―e was allus as good as gowd.

Eh, but ïŋ―eïŋ―d fight wiïŋ― a will when ïŋ―e fowt; ïŋ―e could howd (3) ïŋ―is oïŋ―n,
Anïŋ― Roïŋ― as the dog as knawïŋ―d when anïŋ― wheere to bury his boane.

Anïŋ― ïŋ―e kep his head hoop like a king, anïŋ― ïŋ―eïŋ―d niver not down wiïŋ― ïŋ―is taïŋ―il,
Fur ïŋ―eïŋ―d niver done nowt to be shaïŋ―med on, when we was iïŋ― Howlaby Daïŋ―le.

Anïŋ― ïŋ―e sarved me sa well when ïŋ―e lived, that, Dick, when ïŋ―e cooms to be deïŋ―d,
I thinks as Iïŋ―d like fur to hev soom soort of a sarvice reïŋ―d.

Fur ïŋ―eïŋ―s moor good sense na the Parliament man ïŋ―at stans fur us ïŋ―ere,
Anïŋ― Iïŋ―d voïŋ―t fur ïŋ―im, my oïŋ―n sen, if ïŋ―e could but stan fur the Shere.

ïŋ―Faïŋ―ithful anïŋ― Trueïŋ―ïŋ―them words be iïŋ― Scripturïŋ―anïŋ― Faïŋ―ithful anïŋ― True
Ull be funïŋ― (4) upoïŋ― four short legs ten times fur one upoïŋ― two.

Anïŋ― maïŋ―ybe theyïŋ―ll walk upoïŋ― two but I knaws they runs upoïŋ― four, (5)ïŋ―
Bedtime, Dicky! but waïŋ―it till tha ïŋ―eïŋ―rs it be strikinïŋ― the hour.

Fur I wants to tell tha oïŋ― Roïŋ― when we lived iïŋ― Howlaby Daïŋ―le,
Ten year sinïŋ―Naïŋ―yïŋ―naïŋ―y! tha mun nobbut hevïŋ― one glass of aïŋ―le.

Straïŋ―nge anïŋ― owd-farranïŋ―d (6) the ïŋ―ouse, anïŋ― belt (7) long afoor my daïŋ―y
Wiïŋ― haïŋ―fe oïŋ― the chimleys a-twizzenïŋ―d (8) anïŋ― twined like a band oïŋ― haïŋ―y.

The fellers as maïŋ―kes them picturs, ïŋ―ud coom at the fall oïŋ― the year,
Anïŋ― cattle their ends upo stools to pictur the door-poorch theere,

Anïŋ― the Heagle ïŋ―as hed two heïŋ―ds stanninïŋ― theere oïŋ― the brokken stick; (9)
Anïŋ― they niver ïŋ―ed seed sich ivinïŋ―s (10) as grawïŋ―d hall ower the brick;

Anïŋ― theere iïŋ― the ïŋ―ouse one nightïŋ―but itïŋ―s down, anïŋ― all on it now
Goïŋ―n into mangles anïŋ― tonups, (11) anïŋ― raïŋ―ved slick thruf by the plowïŋ―

Theere, when the ïŋ―ouse wur a house, one night I wur sittinïŋ― aloïŋ―n,
Wiïŋ― Roïŋ―ver athurt my feeïŋ―t, anïŋ― sleeïŋ―pin still as a stoïŋ―n,

Of a Christmas Eïŋ―ve, anïŋ― as cowd as this, anïŋ― the midders (12) as white,
An the fences all on ïŋ―em bolster ïŋ―d oop wiïŋ― the windle (13) that night;

Anïŋ― the cat wur a-sleeïŋ―pin alongside Roïŋ―ver, but I wur awaïŋ―ke,
Anïŋ― smoïŋ―kinïŋ― anïŋ― thinkinïŋ― oïŋ― thingsïŋ―Doïŋ―nt maïŋ―ke thysen sick wiïŋ― the caïŋ―ke.

Fur the men ater supper ïŋ―ed sung their songs anïŋ― ïŋ―ed ïŋ―ed their beer,
Anïŋ― ïŋ―ed goïŋ―n their waïŋ―ys; ther was nobbut three, anïŋ― noïŋ―n on ïŋ―em theere.

They was all on ïŋ―em fearïŋ―d oïŋ― the Ghoïŋ―st anïŋ― dussnïŋ―t not sleeïŋ―p iïŋ― the ïŋ―ouse,
But Dicky, the Ghoïŋ―st moïŋ―stlins (14) was nobbut a rat or a mouse.

Anïŋ― I looïŋ―kt out wonst (15) at the night, anïŋ― the daïŋ―le was all of a thaw,
Fur I seed the beck coominïŋ― down like a long black snaïŋ―ke iïŋ― the snaw,

Anïŋ― I heïŋ―rd greïŋ―t heïŋ―ps oïŋ― the snaw slushinïŋ― down froïŋ― the bank to the beck,
Anïŋ― then as I stood iïŋ― the doorwaïŋ―y, I feeïŋ―ld it drip oïŋ― my neck.

Saw I turnïŋ―d in ageïŋ―n, anïŋ― I thowt oïŋ― the good owd times ïŋ―at was goan,
Anïŋ― the munney they maïŋ―de by the war, anïŋ― the times ïŋ―at was coominïŋ― on;

Fur I thowt if the Staïŋ―te was a gawinïŋ― to let in furrinersïŋ― wheat,
Howiver was British farmers to stanïŋ― ageïŋ―n oïŋ― their feeïŋ―t.

Howiver was I fur to find my rent anïŋ― to païŋ―y my men?
Anïŋ― all along oïŋ― the feller (16) as turnïŋ―d ïŋ―is back of hissen.

Thou slep iïŋ― the chaumber above us, we couldnïŋ―t haïŋ― ïŋ―eard tha call,
Sa Moother ïŋ―ed tellïŋ―d ma to bring tha down, anïŋ― thy craïŋ―dle anïŋ― all;

Fur the gell oïŋ― the farm ïŋ―at slep wiïŋ― tha then ïŋ―ed gotten wer leïŋ―ve,
Fur to goïŋ― that night to ïŋ―er foïŋ―lk by cause oïŋ― the Christmas Eïŋ―ve;

But I cleïŋ―n forgot tha, my lad, when Moother ïŋ―ell gotten to bed,
Anïŋ― I slep iïŋ― my chair hup-on-end, anïŋ― the Freeïŋ― Traïŋ―de runnïŋ―d ïŋ―i my ïŋ―ead,

Till I dreïŋ―mïŋ―d ïŋ―at Squire walkt in, anïŋ― I says to him ïŋ―Squire, yaïŋ―re laïŋ―te,ïŋ―
Then I seed at ïŋ―is faïŋ―ce wur as red as the Yule-block theer iïŋ― the graïŋ―te.

Anïŋ― ïŋ―e says ïŋ―can ya païŋ―y me the rent to-night?ïŋ― anïŋ― I says to ïŋ―im ïŋ―Noïŋ―,ïŋ―
Anïŋ― ïŋ―e cotchïŋ―d howd hard oïŋ― my hairm, (17) ïŋ―Then hout to-night tha shall goïŋ―.ïŋ―

ïŋ―Thaïŋ―ll niver,ïŋ― says I, ïŋ―be a-turnin ma hout upoïŋ― Christmas Eïŋ―veïŋ―?
Then I waïŋ―ked anïŋ― I fun it was Roïŋ―ver a-tugginïŋ― anïŋ― tearinïŋ― my slieïŋ―ve.

Anïŋ― I thowt as ïŋ―eïŋ―d goïŋ―n cleïŋ―n-wud, (18) fur I noïŋ―waïŋ―ys knawïŋ―d ïŋ―is intent;
Anïŋ― I says ïŋ―Git awaïŋ―y, ya beïŋ―st,ïŋ― anïŋ― I fetcht ïŋ―im a kick anïŋ― ïŋ―e went.

Then ïŋ―e tummled up stairs, fur I ïŋ―eïŋ―rd ïŋ―im, as if ïŋ―eïŋ―d ïŋ―a brokken ïŋ―is neck,
Anïŋ― Iïŋ―d cleïŋ―r forgot, little Dicky, thy chaumber door wouldnïŋ―t sneck; (19)

Anïŋ― I slepïŋ― iïŋ― my chair ageïŋ―n wiïŋ― my hairm hinginïŋ― down to the floor,
Anïŋ― I thowt it was Roïŋ―ver a-tugginïŋ― anïŋ― tearinïŋ― me wuss nor afoor,

Anïŋ― I thowt ïŋ―at I kickïŋ―d ïŋ―im ageïŋ―n, but I kickïŋ―d thy Moother istead.
ïŋ―What arta snorinïŋ― theere fur? the house is afire,ïŋ― she said.

Thy Moother ïŋ―ed beïŋ―n a-nagginïŋ― about the gell oïŋ― the farm,
She offens ïŋ―ud spy summut wrong when there warnïŋ―t not a mossel oïŋ― harm;

Anïŋ― she didnïŋ―t not solidly meïŋ―n I wur gawinïŋ― that waïŋ―y to the bad,
Fur the gell (20) a was as howry a trollope as iver traïŋ―pesïŋ―d iïŋ― the squad.

But Moother was free of ïŋ―er tongue, as I offens ïŋ―ev tellïŋ―d ïŋ―er mysen,
Sa I kep iïŋ― my chair, fur I thowt she was nobbut a-rilinïŋ― ma then.

Anïŋ― I says ïŋ― Iïŋ―d be good to tha, Bess, if thaïŋ―d onywaïŋ―ys let ma be good,ïŋ―
But she skelpt ma haïŋ―fe ower iïŋ― the chair, anïŋ― screeïŋ―d like a Howl gone wud (21)ïŋ―

ïŋ―Ya mun run fur the lether. (22) Git oop, if yaïŋ―re onywaïŋ―ys good for owt.ïŋ―
And I says ïŋ―If I beïŋ―nt noïŋ―waïŋ―ysïŋ―not nowadaïŋ―ysïŋ―good fur nowtïŋ―

Yit I beïŋ―nt sich a Nowt (23) of all Nowts as ïŋ―ull hallus do as ïŋ―eïŋ―s bid.ïŋ―
ïŋ―But the stairs is afire,ïŋ― she said; then I seed ïŋ―er a-cryinïŋ―, I did.

Anïŋ― she beïŋ―ld ïŋ―Ya mun saïŋ―ve little Dick, anïŋ― be sharp about it anïŋ― all,ïŋ―
Sa I runs to the yard fur a lether, anïŋ― sets ïŋ―im ageïŋ―n the wall,

Anïŋ― I claums anïŋ― I mashes the winder hin, when I gits to the top,
But the heat druv hout iïŋ― my heyes till I feïŋ―ld mysen ready to drop.

Thy Moother was howdinïŋ― the lether, anïŋ― tellinïŋ― me not to be skeïŋ―rd,
Anïŋ― I wasnïŋ―t afeïŋ―rd, or I thinks leïŋ―st-waïŋ―ys as I wasnïŋ―t afeïŋ―rd;

But I couldnïŋ―t see fur the smoïŋ―ke wheere thou was a-liggin, my lad,
Anïŋ― Roïŋ―ver was theere iïŋ― the chaumber a-yowlinïŋ― anïŋ― yaupinïŋ― like mad;

Anïŋ― thou was a-beïŋ―linïŋ― likewise, anïŋ― a-squeïŋ―linïŋ―, as if tha was bit,
Anïŋ― it wasnïŋ―t a bite but a burn, fur the merkïŋ―s (24) oïŋ― thy shouïŋ―der yit;

Then I callïŋ―d out Roïŋ―, Roïŋ―, Roïŋ―, thaw I didnïŋ―t haïŋ―fe think as ïŋ―eïŋ―d ïŋ―ear,
But ïŋ―e coomïŋ―d thruf the fire wi my bairn iïŋ― ïŋ―is mouth to the winder theere!

He coomïŋ―d like a Hangel oïŋ― marcy as soon as ïŋ―e ïŋ―eard ïŋ―is naïŋ―me,
Or like tother Hangel iïŋ― Scriptur ïŋ―at summun seed iïŋ― the flaïŋ―me,

When summun ïŋ―ed haxïŋ―d fur a son, anïŋ― ïŋ―e promised a son to she,
Anïŋ― Roïŋ― was as good as the Hangel iïŋ― saïŋ―vinïŋ― a son fur me.

Sa I browt tha down, anïŋ― I says ïŋ―I mun gaw up agean fur Roïŋ―.ïŋ―
ïŋ―Gaw up ageïŋ―n fur the varmint?ïŋ― I tellïŋ―d ïŋ―er ïŋ―Yeïŋ―s I mun goïŋ―.ïŋ―

Anïŋ― I claumbïŋ―d up ageïŋ―n to the winder, anïŋ― clemmïŋ―d (25) owd Roïŋ― by the ïŋ―eïŋ―d,
Anïŋ― ïŋ―is ïŋ―air coomïŋ―d off iïŋ― my ïŋ―ands anïŋ― I taïŋ―ked ïŋ―im at fust fur dead;

Fur ïŋ―e smellïŋ―d like a herse a-singeinïŋ―, anïŋ― seeïŋ―mïŋ―d as blind as a poop,
Anïŋ― haïŋ―fe on ïŋ―im bare as a bublinïŋ―. (26) I couldnïŋ―t wakken ïŋ―im oop,

But I browt ïŋ―im down, anïŋ― we got to the barn, fur the barn wouldnïŋ―t burn
Wiïŋ― the wind blawinïŋ― hard tother waïŋ―y, anïŋ― the wind wasnïŋ―t like to turn.

Anïŋ― I kep a-callinïŋ― oïŋ― Roïŋ― till ïŋ―e waggled ïŋ―is taïŋ―il fur a bit,
But the cocks kep a-crawinïŋ― anïŋ― crawinïŋ― all night, anïŋ― I ïŋ―ears ïŋ―em yit;

Anïŋ― the dogs was a-yowlinïŋ― all round, and thou was a-squeïŋ―linïŋ― thysen,
Anïŋ― Moother was nagginïŋ― anïŋ― groïŋ―ninïŋ― an moïŋ―ninïŋ― anïŋ― nagginïŋ― ageïŋ―n;

Anïŋ― I ïŋ―eïŋ―rd the bricks anïŋ― the baulks (27) rummle down when the roof gev waïŋ―y,
Fur the fire was a-raïŋ―ginïŋ― anïŋ― raïŋ―vinïŋ― anïŋ― roïŋ―rinïŋ― like judgment daïŋ―y.

Warm enew theere sewer-ly, but the barn was as cowd as owt,
Anïŋ― we cuddled and huddled togither, anïŋ― happt (28) wersens oop as we mowt.

Anïŋ― I browt Roïŋ― round, but Moother ïŋ―ed beïŋ―n sa soïŋ―kïŋ―d wiïŋ― the thaw
ïŋ―At she cotchïŋ―d ïŋ―er death oïŋ― cowd that night, poor soul, iïŋ― the straw.

Haïŋ―fe oïŋ― the parish runnïŋ―d oop when the rigtree (29) was tummlinïŋ― inïŋ―
Too laïŋ―teïŋ―but itïŋ―s all ower nowïŋ―hall howerïŋ―anïŋ― ten year sin;

Too laïŋ―te, tha mun git tha to bed, but Iïŋ―ll coom anïŋ― Iïŋ―ll squench the light,
Fur we moïŋ―nt ïŋ―ev naw moor firesïŋ―and soa little Dick, good-night.
meaning:
About: Alfred Tennyson, 1st Baron Tennyson (6 August 1809 ïŋ― 6 October 1892) was Poet Laureate of the United Kingdom and is one of the most popular English poets.

Much of his verse was based on classical mythological themes, although In Memoriam was written to commemorate his best friend Arthur Hallam, a fellow poet and classmate at Trinity College, Cambridge who was engaged to Tennyson's sister but died from a cerebral hemorrhage. One of Tennyson's most famous works is Idylls of the King (1885), a series of narrative poems based entirely on King Arthur and the Arthurian tales, as thematically suggested by Sir Thomas Malory's earlier tales on the legendary king. The work was dedicated to Prince Albert, the husband of Queen Victoria. During his career, Lord Tennyson attempted drama, but his plays enjoyed little success even in his lifetime.

Tennyson wrote a number of phrases that have become commonplaces of the English language, including: "nature, red in tooth and claw", "better to have loved and lost", "Theirs not to reason why, Theirs but to do and die", and "My strength is as the strength of ten, Because my heart is pure".

He is the second most frequently quoted writer in the English language, after Shakespeare.
